title: Business Support Organizations Available to Help You
objectives: Identify SBDC, SBA, chambers of commerce, and economic development agencies relevant to their business
Describe what each organization offers (counseling, funding access, networking, training)
Explain how to access these resources and what to expect from the engagement

contentBlocks.title: Discovering the Springs, Where Everything Connects
contentBlocks.content:
Imagine stepping off a trail in Central Florida and discovering a crystal-clear natural spring. The water is so transparent you can see every detail of the riverbed below. Fish, turtles, manatees, cypress roots, aquatic plants: an entire ecosystem thriving because everything in it is interconnected. Central Florida's business support network works the same way. SBDC, SBA, chambers of commerce, UCF BIP, SCORE, Prospera: each one feeds into the same living system, and they are all here to support founders like you.

A Colombian founder spent $4,500 on a business consultant to put together a market entry plan for Central Florida. Three months later, she discovered the SBDC at UCF offers the same service, for free. She had been standing at the edge of the springs without realizing the whole ecosystem was already there.

What does that mean for you? The U.S. has a full network of business support groups funded by federal, state, and local government. Most welcome foreign founders, many have bilingual staff, and almost all are free or low-cost. You are not asking for charity. These resources exist specifically for founders like you.

contentBlocks.title: Free Help Is Closer Than You Think
contentBlocks.content:
A founder from Colombia spent $4,500 on a private consultant to help navigate U.S. business setup — then discovered that the Small Business Development Center at UCF offers the same guidance for free. Central Florida has several organizations specifically designed to help international founders, and most of them cost nothing.

Here are the five you should know. Each one serves a different purpose — explore the directory below to find the right fit for where you are right now.

contentBlocks.title: Resource Directory — What Each Organization Does

sections.heading: Florida SBDC at UCF
sections.content:
**What they do:** Free, confidential business consulting. Market research, financial projections, business plan review.

**Best for:** International founders who need hands-on guidance setting up operations.

**How to access:** Schedule a consultation at floridaSBDC.org. No cost, no obligation.

**International-friendly:** Yes — they work extensively with international entrepreneurs.

sections.heading: SBA (Small Business Administration)
sections.content:
**What they do:** Federal agency providing loan guarantees, training, and contracting assistance.

**Best for:** Accessing capital (SBA-backed loans have better terms than conventional loans).

**How to access:** sba.gov — find your local SBA district office.

**International-friendly:** Loans require U.S. residency, but training is open to all.

sections.heading: Orlando Economic Partnership
sections.content:
**What they do:** Regional economic development organization. Business relocation assistance, site selection, incentive coordination.

**Best for:** Companies planning to physically relocate to Central Florida.

**How to access:** orlando.org — request a consultation for business expansion.

**International-friendly:** Yes — they have a dedicated international business team.

sections.heading: Prospera
sections.content:
**What they do:** Bilingual business development services specifically for Hispanic entrepreneurs.

**Best for:** Spanish-speaking founders who want support in their native language.

**How to access:** prosperausa.org — free workshops and 1-on-1 consulting.

**International-friendly:** Specifically designed for immigrant entrepreneurs.

sections.heading: SCORE Orlando
sections.content:
**What they do:** Free mentoring from retired business executives. Workshop series on startup fundamentals.

**Best for:** Founders who want ongoing mentorship from experienced entrepreneurs.

**How to access:** score.org/orlando — match with a mentor.

**International-friendly:** Yes, but mentoring is in English.

contentBlocks.title: Your Central Florida Resource Network
contentBlocks.titleEs: Tu Red de Recursos en Florida Central

cards.heading: UCF Business Incubation Program
cards.desc: Free mentoring, workshops, office space for startups. Located at UCF campus and East Orlando.
cards.descEs: Mentoría gratuita, talleres, espacio de oficina para startups. Ubicado en el campus UCF y este de Orlando.

cards.heading: Small Business Development Center (SBDC)
cards.headingEs: Centro de Desarrollo de Pequeñas Empresas (SBDC)
cards.desc: Free 1:1 consulting, business plan help, market research. Downtown Orlando and multiple locations.
cards.descEs: Consultoría 1:1 gratuita, ayuda con plan de negocios, investigación de mercado. Downtown Orlando y múltiples ubicaciones.

cards.heading: SCORE Orlando
cards.desc: Free mentoring from retired business executives. Extensive experience across industries.
cards.descEs: Mentoría gratuita de ejecutivos de negocios jubilados. Amplia experiencia en diversas industrias.

cards.heading: Enterprise Florida
cards.desc: State organizational trade assistance, export development. Helps international businesses enter the U.S. market.
cards.descEs: Asistencia estatal de comercio organizacional, desarrollo de exportaciones. Ayuda a negocios internacionales a entrar al mercado de EE.UU.

cards.heading: Orlando Economic Partnership
cards.desc: Regional economic development, relocation assistance, industry connections. Lake Nona and Downtown.
cards.descEs: Desarrollo económico regional, asistencia de reubicación, conexiones industriales. Lake Nona y Downtown.

contentBlocks.footer: All organizations listed offer FREE or low-cost services for international entrepreneurs.
contentBlocks.footerEs: Todas las organizaciones listadas ofrecen servicios GRATIS o de bajo costo para emprendedores internacionales.

contentBlocks.title: Did You Know?
contentBlocks.content: Florida has more than 700 natural springs, the largest concentration of freshwater springs on Earth. Many of these springs discharge millions of gallons of water every day, feeding rivers, wetlands, and entire ecosystems downstream. The business support network in Central Florida works in a similar way: federal funding flows into the SBA, which feeds the SBDC, which connects to chambers and programs like UCF BIP. One resource leads to the next, and the whole system stays healthy because the parts are connected.

contentBlocks.title: Watch Your Money: What This Actually Costs
contentBlocks.content:
Notice how affordable this ecosystem is. Support organization costs as of early 2026:

  • SBDC business counseling: Free (unlimited sessions)
  • SBDC workshops: Free to $50 per session
  • SBA.gov resources and guides: Free
  • Orlando Economic Partnership consultation: Free
  • Chamber of Commerce membership: $200–$500/year
  • SCORE mentoring: Free (volunteer business mentors)
  • Prospera programs: Free to low-cost

Compare that to private help: $150–$350/hour for a business consultant, or $3,000–$10,000 for a market entry strategy that the SBDC develops with you at no cost. Always check the free options first.

contentBlocks.title: Common Mistakes International Founders Make — Resources
contentBlocks.content:
Why does this matter? Consider these common pitfalls:

  • Not knowing about free resources (SBDC, SBA counseling). Most international founders assume quality business advice costs money. The U.S. has an entire taxpayer-funded system built to help you for free.
  • Paying consultants for advice available through government programs. Before you hire any consultant, check if the SBDC or SBA covers that topic first. You might save $5,000 or more.
  • Not leveraging UCF BIP and chamber connections. These organizations exist to connect you with the right people. A warm introduction from UCF BIP or a chamber contact carries more weight than a cold email.

questions.stem: Which organization provides free, ongoing one-on-one business counseling to international founders in Central Florida?
questions.explanation: The Florida SBDC (Small Business Development Center) at UCF provides free one-on-one business counseling. This is an ongoing relationship, not a one-time meeting. Advisors help with business planning, market research, financial analysis, and strategy at no cost.

questions.stem: An international founder wants help with market research and a business plan. What should they do FIRST?
options: Hire a business consultant specializing in market entry
Join the Orlando Regional Chamber of Commerce
Apply for an SBA loan to fund the research
Schedule a free appointment with the Florida SBDC at UCF
questions.explanation: The SBDC should be your first stop for market research and business planning support. Their advisors provide exactly this service at no cost. Hiring a consultant for the same work could cost $3,000–$10,000. Always check free resources before paying for private help.

questions.stem: A founder from Peru just arrived in Central Florida. She needs help with market research, a business plan, and connections to potential customers. She has $8,000 set aside for consulting fees. Based on how the support ecosystem works, what should she expect when she engages with these organizations?
options: She will need to pay the $8,000 to access quality business advising through the SBDC and SCORE
Each organization will provide identical services, so she only needs to contact one

The SBDC will provide free ongoing counseling for her business plan and market research, SCORE will match her with an industry mentor, and a chamber of commerce will connect her to networking events — allowing her to save most of that $8,000 for actual business operations
These organizations only serve U.S. citizens, so she will need to hire a private consultant instead
questions.explanation: The support ecosystem is interconnected but each organization serves a distinct role. The SBDC provides free, unlimited business counseling including market research and business plans. SCORE offers free mentorship from experienced executives. Chambers of commerce provide networking for a modest annual fee ($200-$500). These services are available to international founders regardless of citizenship status. The $8,000 she budgeted for consulting could be redirected to actual business operations.

contentBlocks.title: Founder meeting with a business advisor at a Central Florida resource center
Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: The takeaway is simple. Central Florida's support ecosystem is like its natural springs: deep, clear, and interconnected. The SBDC at UCF should be your first call for business planning and market research. The SBA offers federal resources and guides. Chambers of commerce provide networking and credibility. Use these resources before you spend money on private consultants. Try this: compare the free options to paid ones, then see which groups match your stage. The founders who succeed fastest are the ones who step into the ecosystem and use every available advantage.
